title: DORA ICT Third-Party SLA Performance Monitoring

compliance.assurance_rationale: DORA Art.30 — Reviewed: SPL provides contributing evidence for this regulatory requirement. The search query directly addresses the monitoring objective specified by the clause.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that DORA Art.30 is enforced — Splunk UC-22.3.14: DORA ICT Third-Party SLA Performance Monitoring.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.3.14' running on index itsi_summary and supporting catalogue-defined data sources, archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

dataSources: `index=itsi_summary` (service/KPI data for provider-dependent services), synthetic monitoring results, `dora_provider_slas.csv`
spl:
index=itsi_summary is_service_in_maintenance=0 earliest=-30d
| lookup dora_provider_slas.csv service_name OUTPUT provider_name, sla_availability_pct, sla_response_ms, contract_id, criticality
| where isnotnull(provider_name)
| stats avg(health_score) as avg_health, count(eval(severity_value>=4)) as critical_breaches, count as total_observations by service_name, provider_name, sla_availability_pct, criticality
| eval actual_availability=round(100*(total_observations - critical_breaches)/total_observations, 2)
| eval sla_met=if(actual_availability >= sla_availability_pct, "MET", "BREACHED")
| where sla_met="BREACHED" OR avg_health < 80
| sort actual_availability
| table service_name, provider_name, criticality, sla_availability_pct, actual_availability, sla_met, critical_breaches, avg_health
description: Article 30 requires contractual arrangements to include precise quantitative and qualitative performance targets for services supporting critical functions. This use case monitors actual ICT provider performance against SLA targets — availability, response time, throughput — detecting SLA breaches that may indicate degraded operational resilience and trigger contractual remediation or exit procedures.
value: Gives the responsible team auditor-ready evidence for DORA compliance by automating regulatory compliance evidence using indexes itsi_summary. Replaces manual evidence gathering with a continuous Splunk search that produces structured, time-stamped artefacts suitable for regulatory examination and internal audit review.
implementation: (1) Map ITSI services to ICT providers and their SLA targets in `dora_provider_slas.csv`; (2) include availability, response time, and throughput SLAs; (3) alert on SLA breaches for critical function providers; (4) escalate repeated breaches for exit strategy activation; (5) report provider performance to management body quarterly. Indexes required: `index=itsi_summary`.
visualization: Table (provider SLA compliance), Gauge (availability per provider), Bar chart (SLA breaches by provider), Line chart (provider health trend). Key fields: `actual_availability`, `avg_health`, `critical_breaches`, `criticality`, `provider_name`, `service_name`.
knownFalsePositives: Planned maintenance, backups, or batch jobs can drive metrics outside normal bands — correlate with change management windows.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Install and configure the required add-on or app: Splunk ITSI (Splunkbase 1841), Splunk Synthetic Monitoring.
• Ensure the following data sources are available: `index=itsi_summary` (service/KPI data for provider-dependent services), synthetic monitoring results, `dora_provider_slas.csv`.
• For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 — Configure data collection
(1) Map ITSI services to ICT providers and their SLA targets in `dora_provider_slas.csv`; (2) include availability, response time, and throughput SLAs; (3) alert on SLA breaches for critical function providers; (4) escalate repeated breaches for exit strategy activation; (5) report provider performance to management body quarterly.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=itsi_summary is_service_in_maintenance=0 earliest=-30d
| lookup dora_provider_slas.csv service_name OUTPUT provider_name, sla_availability_pct, sla_response_ms, contract_id, criticality
| where isnotnull(provider_name)
| stats avg(health_score) as avg_health, count(eval(severity_value>=4)) as critical_breaches, count as total_observations by service_name, provider_name, sla_availability_pct, criticality
| eval actual_availability=round(100*(total_observations - critical_breaches)/total_observations, 2)
| eval sla_met=if(actual_availability >= sla_availability_pct, "MET", "BREACHED")
| where sla_met="BREACHED" OR avg_health < 80
| sort actual_availability
| table service_name, provider_name, criticality, sla_availability_pct, actual_availability, sla_met, critical_breaches, avg_health
```

Understanding this SPL

**DORA ICT Third-Party SLA Performance Monitoring (Art. 30)** — Article 30 requires contractual arrangements to include precise quantitative and qualitative performance targets for services supporting critical functions. This use case monitors actual ICT provider performance against SLA targets — availability, response time, throughput — detecting SLA breaches that may indicate degraded operational resilience and trigger contractual remediation or exit procedures.

Documented **Data sources**: `index=itsi_summary` (service/KPI data for provider-dependent services), synthetic monitoring results, `dora_provider_slas.csv`. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): Splunk ITSI (Splunkbase 1841), Splunk Synthetic Monitoring. The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: itsi_summary.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Scopes the data: index=itsi_summary, time bounds. Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
• Enriches events using `lookup` (lookup definition + optional OUTPUT fields).
• Filters the current rows with `where isnotnull(provider_name)` — typically the threshold or rule expression for this monitoring goal.
• `stats` aggregates events by **service_name, provider_name, sla_availability_pct, criticality**, producing one row per unique combination for trend analysis and threshold evaluation.
• `eval` defines or adjusts **actual_availability** — applying business logic to classify, threshold, or derive compliance-relevant indicators.
• `eval` defines or adjusts **sla_met** — applying business logic to classify, threshold, or derive compliance-relevant indicators.
• Filters the current rows with `where sla_met="BREACHED" OR avg_health < 80` — typically the threshold or rule expression for this monitoring goal.
• Orders rows with `sort` — combine with `head`/`tail` for top-N patterns.

Output fields: actual_availability, avg_health, critical_breaches, criticality, provider_name, service_name, sla_availability_pct, sla_met.

Step 3 — Validate
Confirm that events are present in the index and that the search returns expected results. Compare with known good/bad scenarios if applicable. Verify field extractions and index permissions.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Table (provider SLA compliance), Gauge (availability per provider), Bar chart (SLA breaches by provider), Line chart (provider health trend).
grandmaExplanation: We keep track of article 30 requires contractual arrangements to include precise quantitative and qualitative performance targets for services supporting critical functions. this use case monitors actual ict provid. This matters because it catches problems that could lead to audit findings or compliance gaps.
